---
title: "Tangerine_part4"
author: "Tangerine"
format: html
editor: visual
---

# Buddy challenge part 4: Bayesian models

DELETE

# Task: delete this later

This is **Part 4** of the buddy challenge: Run a series of Bayesian models equivalent to the critical model in the original article that originally did not converge:

-   \(a\) run the **closest equivalent of their analysis** (i.e. same structure but using flat priors)

-   \(b\) run a **proper model with proper, weakly informative priors** and justify your prior choices;

-   interpret the results relative to the research question by interpreting the posteriors relative to zero;

-   write up the results and write a report on the comparison between your reanalysis and their original analysis. Write **at least 500 words** in which you describe your analysis including your prior choices and compare the results between your analysis and the original analysis both quantitatively and qualitatively (do the analyses arrive at the same conclusions?). 

-   add the report to your GitHub Repo from Part 3.

Each buddy group leader additionally submits their report as a **markdown (.qmd) and knitted pdf file here.**

Naming convention for files: groupname_part4

END OF THE PART THAT NEEDS TO BE DELETED

## Seting up working directory, packages and data

```{r, packages and workind directory}

# just run this code chunk
# function to ignoring the setting of the relative path below when knitting
run_if_not_knitting <- function(expr) {
  if (!isTRUE(getOption("knitr.in.progress"))) {
    eval(expr)
  }
}

# nifty code using the pacman package
# it checks if the packages specified below are installed, if not, 
#they will be installed, if yes, they will be loaded
if (!require("pacman")) install.packages("pacman")
pacman::p_load(rstudioapi, tidyverse, bayesrules,
               tidybayes, trimr, brms, bayesplot, bayestestR, modelr)

# set the current working directory to the one where this file is
run_if_not_knitting(current_working_dir <- dirname(rstudioapi::getActiveDocumentContext()$path))
run_if_not_knitting(setwd(current_working_dir))

# options to increase efficiency of brms models (optional)
#options(brms.backend = "cmdstanr")
options(mc.cores = parallel::detectCores())


```

```{r, data loading}
#| label: prep data for task 1
#| echo: false 
aud_raw <- read_csv("reanalysis/data_auditory.csv")

aud  <-  aud_raw |>
  # excluding speech errors
  filter(accuracy == 1) |>
  # excluding empty audio files
  filter(rt > 0) |> 
  # excluding RT below 250ms
  sdTrim(minRT = 250,
         # excluding RT above mean+3*sd per participant
         sd = 3,
         perCondition = FALSE,
         perParticipant = TRUE,
         returnType = "raw") |>
  # Changing the name of the column "sound" 
  # to match the original naming in Mascelloni et al
  mutate(distractor_word = sound) |>
  # removing the column named sound
  select(-sound) |>
  # creating categories needed for the model
  mutate(distractor_type = case_when(condition == "category" ~ "Categorical",
                                     condition == "unrelated_cat" ~ "Categorical",
                                     condition == "mediated" ~ "Mediated",
                                     condition == "unrelated_med" ~ "Mediated"),
         # creating a column for distractor relatedness
         distractor_relatedness = case_when(condition == "category" ~ "related",
                                 condition == "mediated" ~ "related",
                                 condition == "unrelated_cat" ~ "unrelated",
                                 condition == "unrelated_med" ~ "unrelated")) |>
  # coding new columns as factors
  mutate(distractor_type = as.factor(distractor_type),
         distractor_relatedness = as.factor(distractor_relatedness)) |>
  # removing unnecessary columns for aesthetics
  select(-condition, -run, -SOA, -accuracy)

# double checking levels
levels(aud$distractor_type)
levels(aud$distractor_relatedness)
# changing contrasts for sum-coding
contrasts(aud$distractor_type) <- c(-0.5, 0.5)
contrasts(aud$distractor_relatedness) <- c(0.5, -0.5)

```

## Bayesian model closest to Mascelloni et al. (2021) with flat (ish) priors

```{r, Closest equivalent of Mascelloni et al}

# Copy pasting the Mascelloni et al maximal model that did NOT converge
lmer.exp1 <- lmer(rt ~ distractor_type * distractor_relatedness + 
                    (1 + distractor_type.num * distractor_relatedness.num | participant) + 
                    (1 + distractor_type.num * distractor_relatedness.num | pic) + 
                    (1 + distractor_relatedness.num | distractor_word), 
                  data=exp1_trimmedData, 
                  control=lmerControl(optimizer="bobyqa"), 
                  verbose=2)

# Getting our priors
maximal <- bf(rt ~ distractor_type * distractor_relatedness + 
                    (1 + distractor_type * distractor_relatedness | participant) + 
                    (1 + distractor_type * distractor_relatedness | pic) + 
                    (1 + distractor_relatedness | distractor_word))

get_prior(maximal, aud, family = "gaussian")

# Setting flat priors

# We're aware we're not technically setting completely flat priors since, 
# as McElreath points out, flat means all values are possible, but we need 
# the computer to work and also negative rt values are absurd

flat_priors <- c(prior(uniform(0, 1800), class = "Intercept"),
                 #not putting anything for class b since the model will
                 #automatically do flat if not told otherwise
                 #we put a broad prior for the sd and sigma which is the closest
                 #we manage to get to fully flat with our machines being able
                 #to run this. We initially had way flatter but it kept "frying"
                 #the computer we were using
              prior(normal(0, 50), class = "sd"),
              prior(normal(0, 50), class = "sigma"))


flat_model_bayesian <- brm(
  formula = maximal,
  data = aud,
  prior = flat_priors,
  family = gaussian(),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000, 
  seed = 42,
  thin = 1,
  cores = 4        
)
# checking convergence diagnostics
mcmc_plot(flat_model_bayesian,
          type = 'trace') 
# looks like healthy caterpillars
mcmc_dens_overlay(flat_model_bayesian,
                  pars = "b_distractor_type1")
mcmc_dens_overlay(flat_model_bayesian,
                  pars = "b_distractor_relatedness1")
mcmc_dens_overlay(flat_model_bayesian,
                  pars = "b_distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1")
# Overlay for the fixed effects estimates look good too

# looking at model output
flat_model_bayesian
# Rhat and ESS values are fine 

# probability of direction
p_direction(flat_model_bayesian)

# computing p-value out of pd of direction so we can compare it to the original paper (we are aware that we are not actually using flat priors and the values we get therefore cant actually be trusted for comparison)

# p-value for b_distractor_type1
pd_to_p(pd = 0.8688, direction = 'two-sided') 
# p-value: 0.2624

# p-value for b_distractor_relatedness1
pd_to_p(pd = 0.9992, direction = 'two-sided')
# p-value: 0.0016

# p-value for b_distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1
pd_to_p(pd = 0.7502, direction = 'two-sided')
# p-value: 0.4996


# In the original analysis with the reduced model, they report p-values of 0.252 for distractor type, 0.001 for relatedness and 0.428 for the interaction term. These values are quite similar to what we got and we get the exact same "significance" which is only relatedness.

```

## Bayesian model with weakly informative priors

```{r, Proper model with weakly informative priors}

# We tested things with this plot and landed on the following numbers:
# Reasoning was to keep things wide and "open"
plot_normal(718, 220)

# Setting weakly informative priors, taken from the normal ploting we just did
#and from R's "get_priors"'s "advice"
weak_priors <- c(prior(normal(718, 220), class = "Intercept"),
                 prior(normal(0, 220), class = "b", coef = "distractor_relatedness1"),
              prior(normal(0, 220), class = "b", coef = "distractor_type1"),
              prior(normal(0, 220), class = "b", coef = "distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1"),
              prior(lkj(1), class = 'cor'),
              prior(cauchy(0, 3), class = 'sd', group = 'distractor_word'),
              prior(cauchy(0, 3), class = 'sd', group = 'participant'),
              prior(cauchy(0, 3), class = 'sd', group = 'pic'),
              prior(normal(0, 138), class = "sigma"))
              

proper_model <- brm(
  formula = maximal,
  data = aud,
  prior = weak_priors,
  family = gaussian(),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  thin = 1, 
  seed = 42,
  cores = 4        
)



```

## Interpreting the results

```{r, interpreting}
#| label: interpret_sim

summary(proper_model)

# The estimate (so average reaction time across all condition combinations)
# is 754 ms with the 95% credible intervals ranging 707-801 ms. 
# For distractor type categorical, the average reaction time is 749 ms,
# with credible intervals ranging 
# while for distractor type mediated, the average reaction time is 759 ms.
# For distractor type related, the average rt is 

proper_model |> 
  gather_draws(b_distractor_relatedness1, b_distractor_type1, `b_distractor_type1:distractor_relatedness1`) |> 
  # plot
  ggplot(aes(x = .value,
             y = .variable)) +
    stat_halfeye() +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0,
               lty = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "posterior estimate for reaction times",
       y = "") +
  theme_minimal()


p_direction(proper_model)


```

-   interpret the results relative to the research question by interpreting the posteriors relative to zero

## Report comparing our reanalysis and the original analysis

-   write up the results and write a report on the comparison between your reanalysis and their original analysis. Write **at least 500 words** in which you describe your analysis including your prior choices and compare the results between your analysis and the original analysis both quantitatively and qualitatively (do the analyses arrive at the same conclusions?)
